referral_details.reason_for_referral:
Ethan is being referred for Section 504 consideration due to a documented diagnosis of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD, combined type) and generalized anxiety that, together, substantially limit his ability to concentrate, organize tasks, manage time, and complete schoolwork within typical classroom structures and timelines.

Over the past year, Ethan’s academic performance has fluctuated significantly despite clear evidence that he is bright and understands the material when given additional time, structure, and verbal prompts. In my English Language Arts class, he often participates meaningfully in discussions and can verbally analyze texts at or above grade level, but his written output during independent work and tests is often incomplete or hastily done. For instance, on a recent in-class essay assignment, Ethan contributed several insightful comments during the pre-writing discussion but turned in only two partially developed paragraphs, leaving three questions completely blank. When I spoke with him privately, he shared that he "zoned out" halfway through, became overwhelmed by how much he had to write, and then felt too anxious to continue.

Ethan’s difficulty with sustained attention is particularly evident during longer tasks (anything over 15–20 minutes). He frequently starts assignments but struggles to follow through without repeated reminders. I often observe him staring at the page, fiddling with objects in his desk, or doodling in the margins instead of working. When redirected, he typically re-engages briefly, but the cycle repeats. He also has trouble following multi-step directions given verbally; if I give a three-step set of instructions, he will often complete only the first step unless I check in with him individually and break it down.

In addition to attention and focus challenges, Ethan shows significant difficulty with organization and time management. His binder and backpack are consistently disorganized, with loose papers crumpled at the bottom, and important handouts or homework assignments frequently go missing. His homework completion rate in my class is around 50–60%, and this is consistent with what his math and science teachers have reported. His mother has emailed me several times explaining that Ethan did complete the homework at home, but then forgot to put it in his backpack or failed to turn it in during class. This pattern has persisted despite repeated coaching and support from both home and school.

Ethan’s anxiety appears to compound these challenges. When he feels behind or overwhelmed, he tends to shut down rather than ask for help. Before quizzes and tests, he often appears visibly tense (fidgeting, leg bouncing, shallow breathing) and has asked on multiple occasions if he can go to the restroom or nurse right before an assessment begins. On two recent occasions, he had what his mother later described as "mild panic attacks" the night before major tests, resulting in difficulty sleeping and coming to school the next day exhausted. In class, when I hand back graded work, Ethan is hesitant to look at his score and has said things like, "I probably failed," even when he actually earned a passing or average grade.

These attention, organization, and anxiety-related issues are impacting Ethan’s access to the general education curriculum in multiple subject areas. His current grades this trimester are B- in English, C in Social Studies, C- in Science, and D+ in Math. His teachers generally agree that his grades do not reflect his actual understanding of the material, but rather his inconsistent completion of work, difficulty with tests under time pressure, and trouble keeping track of assignments and deadlines. Without accommodations that address his functional limitations, he is at risk of falling further behind academically and experiencing increasing emotional distress related to school.

Ethan’s parents have shared that he was formally diagnosed with ADHD by a pediatric neurologist in 2022 and that he has been seeing a child therapist for anxiety since early 2024. While his family is very supportive and has implemented routines at home (such as a dedicated homework space and nightly backpack checks), these supports alone have not been sufficient to fully address the difficulties he experiences in the school environment. His challenges are chronic and have persisted across multiple school years and teachers.

Based on these observations and the information provided by Ethan’s family, I believe that Ethan’s ADHD and anxiety substantially limit major life activities related to learning, concentrating, thinking, and organizing tasks. He would benefit from a formal Section 504 plan to ensure consistent accommodations across all of his classes, to reduce the impact of his disability on his academic performance, and to support his emotional well-being at school.
referral_details.accommodations_and_interventions_attempt:
A variety of classroom-level accommodations and informal interventions have been attempted over the past several months to support Ethan, with mixed and often inconsistent results. Below is a summary of what has been tried, along with observations about how effective these strategies have been:

1. Preferential seating and environmental supports:
- I moved Ethan from the back row to a front-center seat, closer to the board and away from high-traffic areas. This has slightly reduced his off-task behavior related to watching peers, but he still becomes distracted by materials in his own desk or by internal thoughts.
- I have allowed him to use a discreet fidget tool (a small stress ball) to help manage restlessness. When used appropriately, this seems to help him sit through longer instruction, but he sometimes becomes more focused on the object than on the lesson, so I have had to remind him about using it purposefully.
- I routinely check in with him quietly at the start of independent work to ensure he understands the directions and has materials out. This has helped with task initiation but has not fully addressed his difficulty sustaining attention through completion.

2. Instructional and organizational supports:
- I provide written directions on the board for multi-step tasks and have begun underlining or numbering steps. When I also verbally walk Ethan through the first one or two steps, he is more likely to get started correctly. However, once the tasks become more complex or lengthy, he still tends to skip steps or leave items incomplete unless I prompt him again midway through.
- For larger assignments (such as multi-paragraph essays or projects), I have broken the work into smaller chunks with interim due dates. For example, I required an outline on Monday, a rough draft on Wednesday, and a final draft on Friday. When I conferenced with him individually at each step, Ethan was able to make progress, but if I did not check in personally, he often missed the mini-deadlines.
- I gave Ethan a color-coded folder system (red for "To Do," blue for "In Progress," green for "Completed/To Turn In") and spent time with him during homeroom organizing his binder. This worked well for about a week, but he struggled to maintain the system independently, and the folders quickly became mixed or misplaced in his backpack.

3. Homework and assignment completion supports:
- I have routinely written homework assignments on the board and verbally reminded Ethan to copy them into his planner at the end of class. On several occasions, I also walked over to his desk and asked him to show me that he had written the assignment down. This increased the number of homework assignments he at least started at home, but he continues to forget to bring completed assignments back to school or to turn them in at the beginning of class.
- For missing assignments, I have allowed late work without penalty within a one-week window and have emailed his mother with a list of what is missing. This has helped recover some grades when his family is able to step in and support him, but the pattern of initial non-completion has continued despite these extensions.

4. Testing and assessment supports (informal):
- I have occasionally allowed Ethan to move to a quieter corner of the room or to the hallway table during quizzes when he seems particularly anxious or distracted. In those instances, he has generally performed somewhat better, but he still struggles to finish within the standard time limit.
- For one major reading comprehension test, I provided an extra 10 minutes at the end when I noticed he was only halfway through at the regular stopping point. With the additional time, he completed all questions and his score improved from what would have been a failing grade to a low B.
- I have also quietly reminded him to "underline key parts of the question" and "check that you answered every question" before turning in tests. When he follows these strategies, his accuracy improves, but he often rushes and forgets to use them unless I prompt him individually.

5. Behavioral and emotional supports:
- I implemented a simple check-in/check-out system with Ethan during homeroom for about four weeks. Each morning, I would briefly ask him about his goals for the day (e.g., "turn in all completed work," "raise hand at least once in class discussion") and at the end of the day I would review how he felt he did. This seemed to help his self-awareness and motivation temporarily, but it was difficult to sustain consistently without a formal plan and coordination with all of his teachers.
- I have offered short breaks when he appears overwhelmed, such as letting him get a drink of water, take a 2-minute movement break, or briefly step into the hallway to regroup. Ethan usually returns more focused after these breaks, but he sometimes feels embarrassed about leaving and has said he doesn’t want his classmates to think he is "in trouble."
- I have tried to use positive reinforcement, such as specific praise when he completes an assignment on time or stays focused for the full work period. He responds well to positive feedback, but his performance remains inconsistent day-to-day.

6. Collaboration with family and other staff:
- I have communicated regularly with Ethan’s mother via email and one in-person conference. She has shared his ADHD diagnosis and anxiety history and has implemented structured routines at home, such as a nightly homework checklist and packing his backpack together in the evening.
- I have spoken informally with the school counselor, who has met with Ethan several times to teach basic coping strategies for anxiety (deep breathing, positive self-talk). Ethan reports that these strategies "sometimes help," but he often forgets to use them in the moment unless prompted.

Despite these varied supports, Ethan continues to experience significant and ongoing difficulty with attention, organization, and anxiety in the school setting. The interventions described above have produced only partial and inconsistent improvement and require a high level of informal, teacher-initiated support that is not sustainable or guaranteed across all of his classes. A formal Section 504 plan would allow us to implement consistent, clearly defined accommodations—such as extended time on tests and major assignments, access to a quieter testing environment, regular check-ins for organization, and written/visual supports for multi-step tasks—so that Ethan can more equitably access the curriculum and demonstrate his true abilities.
